4 min read
4 hours ago

--

I built a web analytics proxy and have watched ad-blocker filter lists closely for years, so I keep meeting the same quiet problem: a team proxied their analytics to “beat ad blockers,” watched the numbers tick up, and assumed it was solved. Months later the recovered data just stopped, with no alert. I wrote the full version on the DataUnlocker blog; here’s the part that matters if you’re about to build one.
Open the docs of almost any analytics product and you’ll find a page on proxying it past ad blockers. The pitch never changes: route the tracking through your own domain so it looks first-party, and the blocker can’t tell it apart from your real code. The problem isn’t whether it works on day one. It almost always does. The problem is the clock that starts the moment you turn it on.
There are two common flavors, and they run down at very different speeds.
The CNAME proxy is already dead
The oldest version, and the one still offered almost everywhere, is a “managed” proxy using a CNAME DNS record: point a subdomain at your vendor, swap one line of script, no server to run. It stopped working in March 2021.
That was when AdGuard started publishing a public, auto-updating list of CNAME-cloaked trackers. AdGuard’s users sit behind its DNS resolver, so when a custom subdomain resolves to a known analytics endpoint, AdGuard sees it and lists it: thousands of entries, the largest of its kind. Those updates flow downstream into other filter lists within about a week. Security researchers had mapped the same DNS trick from the tracking side too.
On Firefox it’s even faster. uBlock Origin uncloaks CNAMEs live, resolving each hostname through the browser DNS API and blocking the request if the CNAME lands on a domain already in its lists, so nobody has to find your specific subdomain first. Brave does the same. The gap between setting up a CNAME and a blocker seeing through it is essentially zero.
The most convincing evidence isn’t mine. It’s the vendors themselves. One open-source analytics company’s own proxy guide admits ad blockers crawl for CNAMEs and block them, so the CNAME gives you no better stats than the default script. Another pulled the feature entirely, explaining that they could pour hundreds of engineering hours into evasion only to be undone by a single line from a filter-list maintainer. A third still ships it but calls it a “vanity domain” that won’t stop blockers. When the people selling the feature tell you it doesn’t work, believe them.
A custom reverse proxy buys months, not safety
The stronger move is a true reverse proxy: serve analytics from your own origin, under your own path, with no CNAME to anything. The request is genuinely same-origin, so both domain filtering and CNAME uncloaking miss it. This works today, and it’s what most vendors now recommend.
The catch is subtle: a URL being on your domain is not the same as it being invisible. Filter lists match more than domains. They match paths and parameters too, regardless of domain. Keep a recognizable shape like /gtag/js or ?tid=G-… and a generic rule catches it anyway. It has happened in practice: in a 2020 proof of concept, a proxied analytics script was still blocked because EasyList carries a generic path rule that fires on any domain, while a different blocker, using a different list, let the same request through. That arbitrariness is the whole point: you don't control the line, and you can't predict it.
So a reverse proxy survives only as long as you keep renaming the path and every part of it that can match a filter, forever, every time a rule generalizes or someone reports your domain by hand. It’s the same machine as the CNAME proxy, just slower.
Could a blocker eventually detect the analytics product behind a renamed proxy from its behavior rather than its URL? As of mid-2026, no shipping blocker does this for a genuinely first-party proxy. The research and the experiments exist, and Manifest V3 cuts the other way by limiting what extensions can inspect. But it’s a direction, not a capability you have to worry about yet. Today you’re caught by a URL pattern you forgot, or a human report. Tomorrow is a different conversation.
What actually lasts
Every proxy moves the tracker somewhere new and waits to be found again. The work is yours, the deadline is theirs.
The only approach that doesn’t decay does the opposite. Instead of hiding the data, make blocking it cost the user: tie the analytics into something the page needs to work, so a blocker can’t strip the tracking without breaking the site, and breaking sites is the one thing blockers work hard to avoid. That’s the idea behind DataUnlocker, and it’s deliberately analytics-agnostic. It isn’t free of trade-offs, which is a longer discussion. I wrote the practical version, including a single-endpoint trick you can do without any product, in a companion piece, and a separate one on why server-side GTM doesn’t fix this.
Building a reverse proxy for analytics isn’t wrong. It’s just borrowed time, and it’s worth knowing the loan’s terms before you build on it.
